birth.historical_notes: Per Tony Hooker pedigree + Sam family knowledge: b.1909 — likely Hillsborough or San Francisco given father Robert Gay's residence pattern

marriages.notes: First wife (per WikiTree + 1940 census + family records). Two sons: Michael G. Hooker (Sam Talcott Hooker's father, b.~1936, NOT BUILT per living rule) and Rodman L. Hooker Jr. (HD3315, 1940-2016). Separated post-1940 census; Nancy went on to Walker then Borland. Rodman Lent later remarried.
Native of Pasadena; previously married Thomas Bunn of Pebble Beach (3 Bunn children). Died June 1, 1969 at home 761 Chiltern Rd Hillsborough age 54; coroner-investigated unattended death.
Third wife. Born Harrisburg PA 1925. Three Sollner children from prior marriage. No children with Rodman. Survived him by 14 years; d. Tampa FL Oct 5, 2008 age 83.

narrative_blocks.header: First name echoes his Shreve grandmother's surname
narrative_blocks.body: His mother Elizabeth Lawton Shreve had carried Rodman as a maternal-line name through her own mother Rebecca Rodman Nichols. His uncle George Rodman Shreve, a partner in the Shreve & Co. SF jewelry firm, also carried it forward.

narrative_blocks.header: Three marriages: Morse, Austin, Sollner
narrative_blocks.body: He married Nancy Morse in July 1934 and had two sons Michael and Rodman Jr. before their post-1940 separation. He later married Jane Elise Austin (d. 1969 in Hillsborough) and Louise Henrichsen Sollner (d. 2008 in Tampa).

cross_connections.display_label: Middle name 'Lent' honors his father's business partner.

narrative_blocks.header: First name echoes his Shreve grandmother's surname
narrative_blocks.body: His mother Elizabeth Lawton Shreve had carried Rodman as a maternal-line name through her own mother Rebecca Rodman Nichols. His uncle George Rodman Shreve, a partner in the Shreve & Co. SF jewelry firm, also carried it forward.

narrative_blocks.header: Three marriages: Morse, Austin, Sollner
narrative_blocks.body: He married Nancy Morse in July 1934 and had two sons Michael and Rodman Jr. before their post-1940 separation. He later married Jane Elise Austin (d. 1969 in Hillsborough) and Louise Henrichsen Sollner (d. 2008 in Tampa).

cross_connections.display_label: Middle name 'Lent' honors his father's business partner.

death.note: Last residence Pebble Beach. Buried Lake Forest Cemetery IL Section C, Block 15, Lot 13.

marriages.notes: Two sons: Michael G. Hooker (Sam Talcott Hooker's father, b.~1936, NOT BUILT per living-person rule) and Rodman L. Hooker Jr. (HD3315, 1940-2016). Per WikiTree: married July 1934. The 1940 census shows them as a couple in Hillsborough San Mateo County with sons Michael (4) and Rodman Jr. (0). Separated some time after the 1940 census; Nancy was with Walker by 1950.
One daughter Anne Walker (NOT BUILT). Walker had a daughter Martha from prior marriage to Alida Douglas Robinson. Walker d. Apr 8, 1966 Pebble Beach.
No children with Borland; he had four children from prior marriage. They lived at Pebble Beach from 1972 until her 2005 death.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of Pebble Beach's founder Samuel Morse
narrative_blocks.body: She was born May 13, 1915 in San Francisco to Samuel Finley Brown Morse and Anne Camden Thompson, four years before her father founded Del Monte Properties at Pebble Beach. By the 1920 census her parents had separated and her mother had married Col. George Richardson.

narrative_blocks.header: Three husbands: Hooker, Walker, Borland
narrative_blocks.body: After her 1940-era separation from Rodman Lent Hooker she married New York socialite banker Kenneth Stewart Walker, who died at Pebble Beach in 1966. She then married William Fuller Borland on March 7, 1967 and lived at Pebble Beach until her 2005 death there.

narrative_blocks.header: Raised in Lake Forest by stepfather George Richardson
narrative_blocks.body: After her parents' 1916 separation she was raised by her mother and stepfather Col. George Adams Richardson in Lake Forest, Illinois from 1920. The family moved to Rancho Santa Fe in the early 1940s where Richardson ran the Rancho Santa Fe Inn.

narrative_blocks.header: Three marriages produced a blended California family
narrative_blocks.body: Nancy married first Rodman Lent Hooker — sons Michael and Rod Jr. — then Kenneth Stewart Walker, with daughter Anne Walker; and finally William Fuller Borland in 1967, who became stepfather to all three of her Hooker and Walker children. The blended family centered on Pebble Beach, where Nancy's father Samuel F.B. Morse 'The Boss' had built the resort community, and where Borland would chair the AT&T Pro-Am tournament in his retirement years.

death.note: Per obituary 2016. Memorial at Episcopal Church of St. Matthew, San Mateo CA Oct 1, 2016.

narrative_blocks.header: Banker across five California institutions
narrative_blocks.body: He worked as a banker at United California Bank in Los Angeles, Middendorff Colgate & Co., Seattle First National Bank, Crocker National Bank, First Interstate Bank, and Wells Fargo. After banking he became chairman of Washington Square Associates and Cambridge Park Properties.

narrative_blocks.header: Marine Corps service from 1958 to 1961
narrative_blocks.body: He served in the United States Marine Corps from 1958 to 1961, between Cate School and his graduation from USC. He kept the 'Pops' nickname from his family throughout his banking career.

narrative_blocks.header: USC Man of Troy and Business School president
narrative_blocks.body: He was elected President of the USC Business School and received the university's Man of Troy Award. He had prepped at Cate School and Menlo School of Business before USC.

narrative_blocks.header: Pebble Beach summers with grandfather 'The Boss'
narrative_blocks.body: Rod's youth carried him between San Francisco, Hillsborough, and Indian Wells — but the family centerpiece was Pebble Beach, where his maternal grandfather Samuel F.B. Morse 'The Boss' had built the resort community itself. Summer visits with The Boss, brother Michael, and their mother Nancy Morse Hooker were the formative geography of two Hooker generations across the California coast.

cross_connections.display_label: Middle name 'Lent' continues his father's tribute to George.

education.notes: Attended in the 1950s under the Cate School name (the school had been The Santa Barbara School in earlier decades).
Later President of USC Business School alumni board; Man of Troy Award recipient.

institutions.institution_blurb: Member of San Francisco's oldest private gentlemen's club.
Member of the historic San Francisco Peninsula country club.

death.note: Per LegacyConnect record cited in obituary lookup; year 2016.

parents.mother_research_notes: Mother Nancy Borland Morse, daughter of Samuel F.B. Morse 'The Boss' (Pebble Beach developer).

marriages.notes: Wedding at the Episcopal Church of St. Matthew; reception at Burlingame Country Club.

narrative_blocks.header: Hole-in-one on the 15th at Cypress Point
narrative_blocks.body: Among the four career holes-in-one Michael notched as a passionate lifelong golfer was one on the famous 15th hole at Cypress Point Club on the Monterey Peninsula. The course is among the most exclusive in American golf, and the 15th — a downhill par-3 against the Pacific cliffs — sits in the most photographed stretch of the game.

narrative_blocks.header: Marine Corps pilot flying jets off Pacific carriers
narrative_blocks.body: After realizing accounting at Penn wasn't for him, Michael joined the US Marine Corps as a pilot. He earned his wings at Pensacola and spent his service flying jets off carriers across the Pacific — a long way from his Hillsborough and Pebble Beach upbringing alongside his maternal grandfather Samuel Finley Brown Morse, the Pebble Beach developer known to family as 'The Boss'.

narrative_blocks.header: The Hook of Burlingame, Pacific Union, Cypress Point
narrative_blocks.body: Friends called him 'the Hook,' and his San Francisco peninsula life ran through three of the region's most exclusive clubs — Burlingame Country Club where he and Connie celebrated their wedding reception in 1995, the Pacific Union Club in San Francisco, and Cypress Point Club on the Monterey Peninsula where he hit one of his four career holes-in-one. Outside the clubs, fly fishing on Henry's Fork of the Snake River was the constant — Michael regularly depleted the trout stock there.

cross_connections.display_label: Carried four-greats-grandfather Bunker's Gay name into the twentieth century

education.notes: Concord, New Hampshire. Active in hockey, football, and track.
Attended but did not graduate — 'realizing accounting wasn't a passion' per his obituary, he left to enlist as a Marine pilot.

career.notes: From small start-ups to larger firms; built lifelong relationships with institutional clients.

military_service.notes: Basic training in San Diego; received aviator's wings at Pensacola. Flew jets off carriers across the Pacific. Post-Korea, pre-Vietnam era.

birth.note: Year derived from age 74 at March 2012 death.

parents.father_research_notes: Father Robert Latham Woodward of San Francisco (per 2012 obituary). Not built into tree.
parents.mother_research_notes: Mother referred to as 'Mrs. Robert Latham Woodward'.

marriages.notes: Her second marriage; Richard's second. Through this marriage Katherine Hooker Shepherd (HD3388) gained Richard P. Mellon as a stepfather.

narrative_blocks.header: Sat on Courtauld Institute and Art Services boards
narrative_blocks.body: Kitty served as a board member of Art Services International — the Washington-based travelling-exhibition organization — and the American Friends of the Courtauld Institute, the U.S. support group for the London art history institute. Her San Francisco art-collecting and museum life ran in parallel to her Mellon-family marriage in Pennsylvania.

parents.father_research_notes: Father David Edward Austen (X02363), Helen's first husband. Earlier research had assumed Lea's father was a 'Hope' due to Helen's birth surname; corrected pilot77 with full Helen biography from compiler family records.

compiler_connection.notes: Lea Hooker is the compiler's mother. She is generation 11 from Rev. Thomas Hooker (maternal Talcott line) and generation 10 from John Talcott.
compiler_connection.path_to_compiler: TD0057 Lea Hooker → Samuel Talcott Hooker (compiler)

narrative_blocks.header: Marriage produced second Hooker-Talcott convergence
narrative_blocks.body: Lea Austen's 1976 marriage to Michael Gay Hooker produced the project's second Hooker-Talcott convergence — joining the gen-10 Talcott descent line with the Hooker descent line into one family. The first historical convergence had occurred more than a century earlier through a nineteenth-century Hooker-Talcott marriage. Their two children inherit dual Hooker-Talcott descent paths.

narrative_blocks.header: Two children share descent through both lines
narrative_blocks.body: Both of Lea's children with Michael Gay Hooker — Samuel Talcott Hooker (the compiler) and Anne Austen Hooker — carry descent_paths_to_thomas=2 in the project's structural data, reflecting their merged Hooker-Talcott ancestry. The dual-line documentation is what motivated the project as a genealogy visualization in the first place.

narrative_blocks.header: Born May eighth nineteen forty-six
narrative_blocks.body: Lea Austen Hooker was born May 8, 1946. She married Michael Gay Hooker in 1976 and they divorced in 1988; she never remarried. The Austen maiden name comes from her father David Edward Austen.

narrative_blocks.header: Perfected the art of Burlingame entertaining
narrative_blocks.body: Connie and Michael spent twenty-three years perfecting San Francisco-Burlingame entertaining together, from large elegant parties to intimate dinners at their longtime home. Michael, who called her 'Baby' and frequently boasted about her accomplishments, joined her in trips to Sun Valley, New York, and Europe with the close friends they kept across two decades of marriage.

parents.mother_research_notes: Michael's first wife; name not yet on record.

Mother Katherine 'Kitty' Woodward Mellon (I00329) — Michael's second wife, born ~1937 SF, died 2012 Stonington CT. After divorcing Michael, Kitty married Richard Prosser Mellon (X02365) of Ligonier PA — making Richard Katherine's stepfather (devoted, per Richard's 2020 obituary).

narrative_blocks.header: Named Shreve for her great-grandmother
narrative_blocks.body: Katherine carries her great-grandmother's surname 'Shreve' as her own middle name. Elizabeth Lawton Shreve married Robert Gay Hooker and was the matriarch of the California Hooker line that produced both Katherine's grandfather Rodman Lent Hooker and her father Michael.

cross_connections.display_label: Carries great-grandmother's surname Shreve as her middle name

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name Shreve carries forward her great-grandmother Elizabeth Lawton Shreve, who married Robert Gay Hooker (H04266) and was the mother of Michael's father Rodman Lent Hooker (HD3246).

cross_connections.display_label: Today's compiler in line of Whittlesey Memorials co-author
cross_connections.notes: Part of the writing-ancestries CC web — kindred family historians who compiled the foundational printed genealogies underlying this project: Henry Martyn Whittlesey's 1855 first Whittlesey family history (HD3790); Charles Barnes Whittlesey's co-compiled Whittlesey Memorials (X02790); Sebastian Visscher Talcott's Talcott Pedigree 1876 (T01819); Cmdr. Edward Hooker's Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker 1909 (H01628); and Sam Hooker's current digital genealogy project (HD3386).

death.note: Home address 761 Chiltern Road Hillsborough. Per SF Chronicle June 2, 1969 obituary.

marriages.notes: Three children Thomas Bunn (East Coast), Mrs. Robert Devlin (SF), Christopher Bunn (Peace Corps) — none built (not Hooker line).
Second marriage of HD3246 following his separation from Nancy Morse. No children with Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Rodman Lent Hooker in 1956
narrative_blocks.body: She married Rodman Lent Hooker in 1956 at age 41 — his second marriage, following his separation from Nancy Morse. Three children of her former marriage to Thomas Bunn of Pebble Beach were her surviving family.

narrative_blocks.header: Coroner investigated her unattended death at Hillsborough
narrative_blocks.body: She died June 1, 1969 at her home 761 Chiltern Road in Hillsborough at age 54. Because no physician attended her death, the coroner investigated the cause.

marriages.notes: First husband, surname Sollner. Three children Gerhard, Richard, Diana — none built (not Hooker line).
Third and final marriage of HD3246 following Jane Austin's 1969 death. No children together. Louise survived Rodman by 14 years.

narrative_blocks.header: Third wife of Rodman Lent Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: She was the third and final wife of Rodman Lent Hooker, who had previously been married to Nancy Morse and Jane Elise Austin. She had three children from a prior marriage but none with Rodman.

narrative_blocks.header: Painter, photographer, gardener, horsewoman of Hillsborough
narrative_blocks.body: Born in Harrisburg, Pennsylvania in 1925, she grew up in Springfield, Tennessee and lived for years in Tallahassee and Hillsborough. She was an accomplished painter, photographer, gardener, and horsewoman.

death.note: Per Tony Hooker pedigree + Sam's data: lived Hillsborough San Mateo County ~10 years before death; lived San Mateo CA in 1939

burial.plot_notes: Cremated at Cypress Lawn Memorial Park Colma but NOT INTERRED there — disposition of cremains uncertain. Per Findagrave: 'Per Cypress Lawn Records, Robert Hooker was cremated at their facility but not interred there. You can call the office at 650-755-0580 to inquire of the disposition of cremains. Perhaps these children of Charles are in the family Mausoleum???'

careers.role: Real estate agent / house broker / rent collector / insurance agent
careers.employer: Hooker & Lent (Robert G. Hooker + George H. Lent)
careers.location: 14 Post Street, San Francisco CA (telephone: Private Exchange 120)
careers.notes: Real estate agency, house brokerage, rent collection, and insurance.

Real estate and investment firm. Founded with brother-in-law George Heydenfeldt Lent (X02298, husband of his sister Bessie H04268) and brother-in-law Eugene Lent. The firm operated through at least 1932 per SF Real Estate Board Yearbook; building referenced in 1916 SF Commercial Club records. Robert G. Hooker was a Pacific-Union Club member (1932 roster) — same as his Lent business partners.
Per 1880s SF directory: 'Hooker Robert G., cashier Am. Sugar Refinery Co., r. 917 Bush' — early-career position before founding Hooker & Lent.

memberships.notes: One of the most exclusive private clubs in the western US. Robert was a member alongside his Lent business partners (his sister Bessie's husband George Heydenfeldt Lent and brother-in-law Eugene Lent).
One of the most exclusive private clubs in the western US. Per SFgenealogy 1932 roster.
Per Bohemian Club Officers & Members 1892 roster: 'HOOKER, Robert G.' — alongside brother G. Osgood Hooker and brother-in-law George H. Lent (married Bessie that same year).

Per Burlingame Country Club roster: 'HOOKER. ROBERT G.. Cunard Bldg.. 503 Market St.. San Francisco. Calif.' — listed alongside several other Hooker family members including 'Lent Hooker' (likely Rodman Lent Hooker HD3246), 'John R. Hooker' (likely John Rodman Hooker HD3243 at Curran & Dwyer), 'Osgood Hooker' (likely Osgood Hooker H05205 — C. Osgood's son), 'Mrs. C. Osgood Hooker' (Ella Wall Goodfellow Hooker, his sister-in-law widow), and 'Robert G. Hooker Jr.' (660 Market Street, Gladding McBean & Co).

narrative_blocks.header: Real estate broker with two brothers-in-law
narrative_blocks.body: He partnered with George and Eugene Lent in the Hooker & Lent firm at 503 Market Street in San Francisco. The firm operated through 1932 and built a long-running family-business network.

narrative_blocks.header: Studied mechanics at Berkeley, ended in real estate
narrative_blocks.body: He took a Ph.B. in Mechanics at the University of California, Berkeley. After early years as cashier at the American Sugar Refining Company, he settled into real estate brokerage.

narrative_blocks.header: Member of San Francisco's three elite clubs
narrative_blocks.body: He belonged to the Bohemian Club from 1892, the Pacific-Union Club, and the Burlingame Country Club. The Hooker family appeared on the Burlingame roster across three generations.

cross_connections.display_label: Brothers-in-law and partners in Hooker & Lent real estate.
Three brothers-in-law ran Hooker & Lent real estate firm.
Bore great-great-grandfather Bunker's Gay name into 1862 San Francisco
was his father-in-law; Founded Shreve & Co.

education.notes: Per Cal Berkeley alumni directory: 'Hooker, Robert Gay, Ph.B. (Mechanics), Real estate, 1117 Pine st, S F'

residences.source: 1880s SF directory (early career, cashier at Am. Sugar Refinery)

Tony Hooker pedigree (descendant chart for Thomas Hooker)

narrative_blocks.header: Three Hooker brothers, three California fortunes
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Gay arrived in San Francisco in spring 1851 and partnered with younger brother John Delos Hooker at Hooker & Co., 117–119 California Street — the hardware fortune that later funded the 100-inch Hooker Telescope at Mount Wilson. Older brother Henry Clay was simultaneously building Arizona's largest cattle empire.

narrative_blocks.header: Joined the Sons of the American Revolution
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Gay Hooker joined the Sons of the American Revolution, formally documenting his descent from a Revolutionary-era family. He died of pneumonia at age 83 on November 19, 1905, at his home at 967 Bush Street in San Francisco. Buried at Mountain View Cemetery in Oakland — the Bay Area's premier Victorian garden cemetery. (A Findagrave geocoding error lists the death location as 'San Francisco del Mar Municipality, Oaxaca, Mexico'; the actual location is San Francisco, California.)

narrative_blocks.header: A homestead chair followed him to San Francisco
narrative_blocks.body: He took a child's side chair from the Hinsdale homestead with him to San Francisco in 1851. The chair returned east after his 1905 death and now sits at the New Hampshire Historical Society in Concord, provenanced back to his great-grandfather Rev. Bunker Gay.

cross_connections.display_label: Carried his great-grandfather Bunker's Gay surname as a middle name

burial.plot_notes: Mountain View Cemetery, Oakland, Alameda County CA (per Findagrave Memorial 255641260).

careers.employer: Hooker & Co. (with brother John Delos Hooker + Isaac H. Cory)
careers.notes: Came to San Francisco in spring 1851; built fortune in hardware import. Hooker & Co. imported hardware, iron, and related goods. Partnered with brother John Delos Hooker (H02709, who later founded the Hooker Telescope at Mount Wilson Observatory).

Per Truman & Hooker partner biography (re. son Osgood): 'Charles G. Hooker, a prominent hardware merchant of California, who came to this State in 1852, and still resides in San Francisco.' Charles arrived CA 1852 — placed him in the second wave of Gold Rush migrants (1849-1852). Hardware merchandise business connects to the broader CA development boom serving miners + settlers.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Grew up on the Hinsdale homestead before sailing to California in 1851; the family chair he took with him now sits at the New Hampshire Historical Society.

bio.extra_names: Tony Hooker pedigree records full first name as 'Arrabella' — formal name 'Arrabella Maria Celia Osgood'. EH 1909 lists her simply as 'Maria Celia (Osgood)' (using middle/Latin name in everyday use).

sources.short: Tony Hooker pedigree (descendant chart for Thomas Hooker)

burial.plot_notes: Cypress Lawn Memorial Park, Colma San Mateo Co CA. Originally buried at Lone Mountain Cemetery, San Francisco — when that cemetery was decommissioned, remains were removed to Cypress Lawn (Findagrave Memorial 65583447).

sources.short: Tony Hooker pedigree (#1010 George Choate Shreve)
sources.page: b.11 May 1829 d.13 Oct 1893; 13th child, 5th son

careers.notes: Founded what Tony Hooker pedigree calls 'the large manufacturing jewelry house of Geo. C. Shreve & Co., well known in the business world and largest establishment of the kind in the West.' Came to San Francisco in 1852 — among earliest gold-rush-era Yankee merchants — and built the firm into the West's premier jeweler. The firm continued post-mortem under family members; his son George Rodman Shreve (1861-1914) was senior member of Shreve, Treat & Eacret jewelers and formerly of Shreve & Co. The Shreve family remained a fixture of SF jewelry retailing into the 20th century.

notable.notable_blurb: Founded Shreve & Co., oldest SF commercial establishment
notable.primary_url_label: Wikipedia: Shreve & Co. (George has no own page; firm article serves as primary)

narrative_blocks.header: Sailed to gold-rush San Francisco with nephew
narrative_blocks.body: George Choate Shreve was born May 11, 1829 in Salem, Essex County MA — the 13th child and 5th son of Isaac Shreve (1779-1829) and Mary Moulton. His father died the same year George was born. As a young man George learned goldsmithing from his older half-brother Benjamin (b.1780, who later returned to Salem and founded Shreve, Crump & Low in Boston in 1869, a firm that still claims to be 'America's Oldest and Finest Jeweler').

narrative_blocks.header: Built largest jewelry house in the West
narrative_blocks.body: Per the Tony Hooker pedigree (which records George's brief biography from late-19th-century San Francisco mercantile sources): George was 'head of the large manufacturing jewelry house of Geo. C. Shreve & Co., well known in the business world and the largest establishment of the kind in the West.' By the 1860s the firm was considered one of the finest silver and goldsmiths in America.

narrative_blocks.header: His firm's earthquake-proof building survived 1906
narrative_blocks.body: Thirteen years after George's 1893 death, the firm — by then run by his son George Rodman Shreve and others — opened in March 1906 a brand-new eleven-story 'earthquake-proof' building at the corner of Post Street and Grant Avenue, San Francisco. One month later, the great earthquake struck. When the dust settled, the Shreve Building was one of the only structures still standing in the firm's neighborhood.

narrative_blocks.header: His daughter married into Hooker mercantile dynasty
narrative_blocks.body: In 1858 George Choate Shreve married Rebecca Rodman Nichols of Salem MA. Their daughter Elizabeth Lawton Shreve (1868-1943) married Robert Gay Hooker on April 18, 1900 — uniting two prominent SF mercantile families: the Hookers in hardware (Hooker & Co.) and the Shreves in jewelry (Geo. C.

bio.extra_names: Per Findagrave for son George Rodman Shreve, after first husband's death she remarried to a Creamer, becoming 'Rebecca Rodman Nichols Creamer'
